title: Marbling on book cloth
subtitle: Transferring a marbled pattern onto bookbinding cloth for decorative covers
excerpt: Book cloth can be marbled using the same carrageenan-bath process as paper, provided it is mordanted correctly and pressed flat immediately after lifting. Marbled book cloth is used for case bindings, spine panels, and decorated boards where a continuous pattern across the cloth gives a result that no printed endpaper can replicate.
sourceNotes: Joseph Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (translated from the German, first published 1885, public domain via Internet Archive), on marbling on cloth and other non-paper surfaces as an extension of the carrageenan-bath technique.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pre-treating book cloth with a potash alum solution to allow marbling pigments to bond with the fabric surface on lifting. The cloth must be mordanted on the face (the exposed woven side) and dried completely before marbling.

The evenness with which a cloth is held during lowering onto the marbling size. Cloth sags more than paper and must be held at multiple points or over a light frame to prevent the centre touching the size before the edges, which traps air and distorts the pattern.

The bonding of liquid acrylic marbling pigments to cloth fibres. Acrylic pigments bond permanently to fabric when heat-set after drying: the finished cloth should be ironed from the back through a cloth to fix the pattern before use in bookbinding.

content.text: The challenge of marbling on book cloth is handling: cloth drapes and sags, while paper holds its shape. A piece of book cloth lowered carelessly onto the size touches in the centre first and the pattern distorts at the point of contact. Controlling
during the lay-down is the critical skill. The
process is the same as for paper.

items.name: Book cloth, cut to tray size
items.notes: Cut the cloth 20 mm larger than the intended marbled area to allow for handling without touching the marbled surface.

items.name: Potash alum mordant
items.notes: 2 tablespoons in 500 ml warm water. Brush onto the face of the cloth and dry flat.

items.name: Carrageenan size, marbling pigments, ox-gall, eyedroppers
items.notes: Prepare as for any carrageenan pattern.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For pressing the marbled cloth flat as it dries.

items.name: Clean board
items.notes: To press the wet cloth flat immediately after rinsing.

content.text: Brush the alum mordant onto the face of the book cloth and allow to dry flat. Cloth that dries on a wire rack or hung will develop slight warps that cause uneven lay-down on the size. Press under a light weight while drying if necessary.
Lay the cloth on the size

Hold the mordanted cloth by two long edges. Have a second person hold the other two edges if the cloth is large. Lower the cloth onto the size with the face down, keeping it taut and parallel to the size surface. Touch the near long edge first, then lower the rest of the cloth in one smooth motion to prevent air pockets. Leave for 30 seconds, then lift and rinse immediately under cold running water.

After rinsing, press the cloth face-down on a clean board with the bone folder to flatten it before it dries. Allow to air-dry flat. Once dry, heat-set the pattern by ironing from the back through a pressing cloth for two minutes at a medium-high cotton setting. The heat bonds the acrylic pigments to the fabric. The cloth is then ready for use in case binding.

items.symptom: The pattern transfers in patches with blank areas
items.cause: Air was trapped under the cloth during lay-down, preventing contact with the size.
items.fix: Lower the cloth more slowly and keep it completely taut. A second pair of hands makes this significantly easier.

items.symptom: The marbled cloth is stiff and boards-like after drying
items.cause: Carrageenan size was not fully rinsed from the cloth.
items.fix: Rinse the cloth more thoroughly under running water, gently working the surface to remove the gelatinous size.
